objectives: Use Big O notation to express and justify the time and space complexity of an algorithm, simplifying expressions to the dominant term.
Compare standard searching and sorting algorithms by how they work and their best, average and worst-case complexities.
Describe and trace algorithms on data structures, including tree traversals and the shortest-path algorithms Dijkstra and A*.

retrieval.q: Which traversal of a binary search tree outputs values in ascending order?
Which data structure does a breadth-first traversal use, and which does depth-first use?
retrieval.a: BFS uses a queue (FIFO); DFS uses a stack (or recursion).

retrieval.q: What is the time complexity of pushing or popping on a stack, and why?
retrieval.a: O(1) — it only acts on the top element, with no traversal.

retrieval.q: State one precondition that must hold before a binary search can be used.

retrieval.a: O(n²) — drop constants and lower-order terms, keeping the dominant term.

keywords.def: A precise, finite sequence of steps to solve a problem.
How an algorithm's time or space requirement grows with input size n (worst case).
The number of operations performed as a function of input size n.
The amount of memory required as a function of input size n.
Solvable in polynomial time or better / only solvable in exponential time or worse.
Break a problem into subproblems, solve them, then combine (e.g. merge sort, quick sort, binary search).
A rule-of-thumb estimate; in A* the estimate h(n) of the remaining cost from a node to the goal.
A heuristic that never overestimates the true remaining cost, guaranteeing A* finds the optimal path.

content.heading: Big O and complexity classes
points: Big O describes the worst-case growth of time (operations) or space (memory) against input size n.
Simplify by dropping constants and lower-order terms: 3n² + 5n + 17 → O(n²), the dominant term wins.
Order of growth: O(1) constant, O(log n) logarithmic, O(n) linear, O(n log n) linearithmic, O(n²) quadratic, O(2ⁿ) exponential.
Tractable = solvable in polynomial time or better; intractable = only exponential O(2ⁿ) or worse.
Always justify a Big O, e.g. 'O(n²) because of the nested loop — each of n passes makes up to n comparisons.'

content.heading: Searching algorithms
points: Linear search: check each element in turn from the start; works on unsorted data; O(n) average and worst, O(1) best.
Binary search: check the middle of a SORTED list, discard the half that cannot contain the target, repeat; O(log n) worst case.
Binary search precondition: the list MUST be sorted so a half can be safely discarded each pass — always state this.
Binary search is O(log n) because halving the search space means doubling n only adds one extra step.

content.heading: Sorting algorithms
points: Bubble sort: repeatedly compare adjacent pairs and swap if out of order; O(n²) average/worst, O(n) best only with a swap flag; stable, O(1) space.
Insertion sort: build a sorted front section, inserting each element by shifting larger ones right; O(n²) average/worst, O(n) best; stable, O(1) space.
Merge sort: divide and conquer, split until single items then merge; guaranteed O(n log n) in all cases but needs O(n) extra space.
Quick sort: pick a pivot, partition smaller-left / larger-right, recurse; O(n log n) average but O(n²) worst case (poor pivot); not stable.
Classic trade-off: merge sort guarantees O(n log n) time but uses O(n) space; quick sort uses O(log n) space but risks O(n²) time.

content.heading: Data-structure operation complexities
points: Stack (LIFO) and queue (FIFO): push, pop, enQueue, deQueue are all O(1) — they act only on the top/ends.
Array: access by index is O(1), but inserting or deleting shifts elements → O(n).
Linked list: traversal and search are O(n); inserting or deleting given the node is O(1) by re-pointing.
Binary search tree: search/insert is O(log n) average if balanced, O(n) worst if degenerate; full traversal is O(n).
A circular queue uses MOD to wrap its pointers and reuse freed space.

content.heading: Tree traversals
points: Pre-order (Node → Left → Right): used to copy a tree or output prefix expressions.
In-order (Left → Node → Right): outputs a BST's values in ascending sorted order.
Post-order (Left → Right → Node): used to delete a tree or evaluate RPN (postfix); it is the depth-first traversal the spec names.
Breadth-first (BFS): visit level by level, left to right, using a queue; finds shortest paths in unweighted graphs.
Mnemonic for the Node position: Pre = first, In = middle, Post = last.

content.heading: Shortest path: Dijkstra and A*
points: Dijkstra uses cost g(n) only, is uninformed, and searches outward in all directions to find shortest paths to ALL nodes.
Dijkstra: always settle the unvisited node with the smallest tentative distance, relax its neighbours, then never update a settled node.
A* uses f(n) = g(n) + h(n), where h(n) is a heuristic estimate of remaining cost to the goal — this is the key difference.
A* directs the search toward one goal, usually exploring fewer nodes and so running faster than Dijkstra.
A* is optimal only if its heuristic is admissible (never overestimates); if h(n) = 0, A* reduces exactly to Dijkstra.

worked_example.title: Tracing Dijkstra's algorithm from node A (undirected weighted graph)
steps: Edges: A–B=4, A–D=1, B–C=2, B–E=5, C–F=3, D–E=2, E–F=1. Set distance A=0 and all others to infinity.
From A, tentative distances are D=1 and B=4. The smallest unvisited is D=1, so settle D=1 (via A).
From D, relax E: 1 + 2 = 3. Smallest unvisited is now E=3, so settle E=3 (via D).
From E, relax F: 3 + 1 = 4, and B via E: 3 + 5 = 8 (worse than 4, keep B=4). Both B=4 and F=4 are smallest; settle B=4 (via A) and F=4 (via E).
From B, relax C: 4 + 2 = 6. From F, C via F: 4 + 3 = 7 (worse, keep 6). Settle C=6 (via B).
Final shortest distances from A: A=0, D=1 (via A), E=3 (via D), B=4 (via A), F=4 (via E), C=6 (via B).
Complexity: a simple-array Dijkstra is O(n²); with a priority queue it is O((V + E) log V). It explores more nodes than A* because it has no heuristic to steer toward a single goal.

task: Trace a bubble sort on [5, 3, 8, 1, 4], writing the full list after each complete left-to-right pass. Then state and justify the worst-case Big O of bubble sort.
challenge: On the same graph, explain how adding an admissible straight-line heuristic h(n) would change the search to A*, and identify which node Dijkstra wastefully settles that A* searching only for goal F might avoid. Justify why A* still returns the optimal path.

exit_ticket.q: What is the worst-case time complexity of quick sort, and what causes it?
exit_ticket.a: O(n²), caused by a poor pivot choice such as already-sorted data producing unbalanced partitions.

exit_ticket.q: Why does merge sort guarantee O(n log n) but use O(n) extra space?
exit_ticket.a: It always divides into log n levels each doing n work to merge (O(n log n)), but merging needs temporary arrays the size of the input (O(n)).

exit_ticket.q: State the f(n) formula used by A* and what each term means.
exit_ticket.a: f(n) = g(n) + h(n): g(n) is the actual cost from the start to n, h(n) is the heuristic estimate of remaining cost to the goal.
